section.what-is.title: What is a word counter?
section.what-is.p1: A word counter is a tool that automatically counts the number of words, characters, sentences, and paragraphs in a given text. Writers, students, SEO professionals, and content creators use word counters to ensure their content meets specific length requirements, whether for academic submissions, blog posts, social media updates, or professional documents.
section.what-is.p2: Unlike simple word counters that only show a word count, wordcountergo.com provides a comprehensive suite of writing analytics including readability scores, keyword density analysis, letter frequency distribution, and social media limit checks — all in real time as you type.
section.what-is.p3: Our tool supports all languages including English, Spanish, French, Chinese, Arabic, Hindi, Japanese, and more. Because everything runs in your browser, your text never leaves your device — making it the most private word counter available online.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions.
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about counting words with wordcountergo.com. Use our free word counter to start counting.
faq.a1: Words are counted using Unicode word boundary detection via the ECMAScript Intl.Segmenter API. This correctly handles all languages including English, Chinese, Arabic, Hindi, Japanese, and more. A word is any sequence of letters, digits, or apostrophes separated by whitespace or punctuation.
faq.q2: Is my text stored on your servers?
faq.a2: No. Your text is processed entirely in your browser. Nothing is sent to any server. If you refresh the page, your text is restored from localStorage on your device for your convenience.
faq.q3: Does it work with languages other than English?
faq.a3: Yes. The tool uses Unicode-aware word segmentation that works with all languages — Spanish, French, German, Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Arabic, Hindi, Russian, and more. Character counting works for any script including CJK and right-to-left languages.
faq.a4: Pages are calculated based on the standard of approximately 250 words per page (single-spaced). This is the most common convention for academic and professional writing.
faq.a5: Readability scores estimate how easy your text is to read. We show three: Flesch Reading Ease (higher = easier, 60-70 is good), Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level (US grade level), and Gunning Fog Index (years of education needed). These are designed for English text.
faq.a6: Reading time uses the average reading speed of 200 words per minute. Speaking time uses 130 words per minute. These are standard estimates used across the web.
faq.a7: No. You can paste or type as much text as you want. The tool handles large documents without any issues.
faq.a8: Yes. The tool is completely free with no ads, no sign-ups, and no hidden limits. No account required.
guide.title: How to Count Words.
guide.subtitle: A complete guide to counting words, characters, and improving your writing with data. Try our free word counter.
guide.what-is.title: What is word counting?
guide.what-is.text: Word counting is the process of determining the number of words in a document or passage. It's one of the most fundamental metrics in writing, used everywhere from academic essays to SEO content creation. A word counter automatically calculates this for you, along with related metrics like character count, sentence count, and reading time.
guide.why.title: Why word count matters
guide.why.academic.title: Academic writing
guide.why.academic.desc: Essays, dissertations, and assignments often have strict word limits. Going over can mean penalties; going under can mean insufficient depth.
guide.why.seo.title: SEO & content
guide.why.seo.desc: Search engines tend to favor comprehensive content. Blog posts around 1500-2500 words often perform better in search results.
guide.why.social.title: Social media
guide.why.social.desc: Twitter has a 280-character limit. Instagram allows up to 2200 characters. Staying within limits is crucial for sharing.
guide.why.professional.title: Professional writing
guide.why.professional.desc: Reports, proposals, and emails benefit from concise writing. Word count helps you stay on track and respect your reader's time.
guide.how.title: How our word counter works
guide.how.text: wordcountergo.com uses the browser's built-in Intl.Segmenter API for accurate word boundary detection across all languages. This means:
guide.how.li1: Words are identified using Unicode rules — not simple space splitting
guide.how.li2: Chinese, Japanese, and Korean text is segmented correctly
guide.how.li3: Arabic and Hebrew (right-to-left) are fully supported
guide.how.li4: Accented characters and special scripts (Cyrillic, Devanagari, etc.) are handled
guide.how.li5: Hyphenated words and contractions count as single words
guide.metrics.title: Key metrics explained
guide.metrics.paragraphs.desc: Blocks of text separated by blank lines
guide.tips.title: Word counting tips
guide.tips.li1: Set a goal. Use the word/character goal feature to track progress as you write.
guide.tips.li2: Check keyword density. Overusing the same word can hurt readability and SEO.
guide.tips.li3: Monitor readability. Aim for a Flesch Reading Ease score of 60-70 for general audiences.
guide.tips.li4: Use case conversion. If you've pasted text with inconsistent capitalization, use the case converter.
guide.tips.li5: Save your work. Our tool auto-saves your text locally so you won't lose it on refresh.
